session.user:
In these files under /home/runner/work/erk/erk/, replace "plan issue" terminology with "plan":
FILES: 1. docs/learned/cli/tripwires.md 2. docs/learned/cli/workflow-commands.md 3. docs/learned/cli/commands/pr-summarize.md 4. docs/learned/cli/learn-plan-land-flow.md 5. docs/learned/cli/pr-rewrite.md 6. docs/learned/cli/slash-command-llm-turn-optimization.md 7. docs/learned/pr-operations/checkout-footer-syntax.md 8. docs/learned/pr-operations/plan-embedding-in-pr.md 9. docs/learned/pr-operations/pr-submit-phases.md 10. docs/learned/pr-operations/pr-validation-rules.md 11. docs/learned/pr-operations/tripwires.md 12. docs/learned/objectives/objective-lifecycle.md 13. docs/learned/objectives/research-documentation-integration.md 14. docs/learned/objectives/objective-roadmap-check.md 15. docs/learned/objectives/roadmap-mutation-patterns.md 16. docs/learned/erk/pr-commands.md 17. docs/learned/erk/tripwires.md 18. docs/learned/tui/data-contract.md 19. docs/learned/tui/plan-row-data.md 20. docs/learned/ci/learn-ci-environment-detection.md 21. docs/learned/workflows/one-shot-workflow.md 22. docs/learned/workflows/tripwires.md 23. docs/learned/configuration/config-layers.md 24. docs/learned/reference/gastown-analysis.md 25. docs/learned/testing/fake-github-api-reference.md 26. docs/learned/documentation/when-to-switch-pattern.md
RULES: - "plan issue" → "plan" (case-sensitive) - "plan issues" → "plans" (case-sensitive) - "Plan Issue" → "Plan" (title case) - "Plan Issues" → "Plans" (title case) - "erk-plan issue" → "erk plan" - "erk-plan issues" → "erk plans" - "plan issue number" → "plan number" - "plan issue body" → "plan body" - "plan issue metadata" → "plan metadata" - "plan issue closure" → "plan closure" - "plan issue dispatch metadata" → "plan dispatch metadata" - PRESERVE "objective issue" unchanged - PRESERVE "GitHub issue" or "GitHub Issue" unchanged - PRESERVE code references like `IssueInfo`, `GitHubIssues` unchanged - PRESERVE `issue_number` variable names in code blocks unchanged
Read each file, find all instances of "plan issue" (case-insensitive), and replace them. Use the Edit tool for each change.
IMPORTANT: Do NOT run git reset or any git commands. Only use Read and Edit tools.
session.assistant: I'll help you replace "plan issue" terminology with "plan" across these files. Let me start by reading and editing each file.
session.tool_result:
1→--- 2→title: Cli Tripwires 3→read_when: 4→ - "working on cli code" 5→--- 6→ 7→<!-- AUTO-GENERATED FILE - DO NOT EDIT DIRECTLY --> 8→<!-- Edit source frontmatter, then run 'erk docs sync' to regenerate. --> 9→<!-- Generated from cli/*.md frontmatter --> 10→ 11→# Cli Tripwires 12→ 13→Rules triggered by matching actions in code. 14→ 15→**Design batch commands that process items despite validation failures** → Read [Batch Exec Commands](batch-exec-commands.md) first. Validate ALL items upfront before processing ANY items. Stop on first validation error. 16→ 17→**Return non-zero exit codes for batch command failures** → Read [Batch Exec Commands](batch-exec-commands.md) first. Always exit 0, encode errors in JSON output with per-item success fields. 18→ 19→**Use OR semantics for batch success (success=true if any item succeeds)** → Read [Batch Exec Commands](batch-exec-commands.md) first. Use AND semantics: top-level success=true only if ALL items succeed. 20→ 21→**Using RuntimeError for expected CLI failures** → Read [CLI Error Handling Anti-Patterns](error-handling-antipatterns.md) first. RuntimeError signals a programmer error (bug in the code), NOT expected user-facing failures. Use UserFacingCliError for expected conditions (missing files, invalid input, precondition violations) that should exit cleanly with an actionable message. 22→ 23→**WORKFLOW_COMMAND_MAP maps command names to .yml filenames** → Read [Workflow Commands](workflow-commands.md) first. command names intentionally diverge from filenames (e.g., pr-rebase → pr-rebase.yml, but plan-implement → plan-implement.yml via DISPATCH_WORKFLOW_NAME constant) 24→ 25→**adding a column to plan list without checking PlanDataTable.\_setup_columns()** → Read [Plan List Provider Pattern](plan-list-provider-pattern.md) first. Column order in list_cmd.py must mirror plan_table.py for consistency between CLI and TUI. Check both files when modifying columns. 26→ 27→**adding a new exec script that produces JSON consumed by another exec script** → Read [Exec Script Schema Patterns](exec-script-schema-patterns.md) first. Define shared TypedDict in packages/erk-shared/ for type-safe schema. Both producer and consumer import from the same schema definition. 28→ 29→**adding a new required gitignore entry** → Read [Upgrade Workflow](upgrade-workflow.md) first. Add to REQUIRED_GITIGNORE_ENTRIES in src/erk/core/init_utils.py:8. The upgrade path in init/main.py automatically syncs these entries. 30→
... [171 lines omitted] ...
33→**adding a new step to the submit pipeline** → Read [PR Submit Pipeline Architecture](pr-submit-pipeline.md) first. Each step must return SubmitState | SubmitError. Use dataclasses.replace() for state updates. Add the step to \_submit_pipeline() tuple. 35→**adding a parameter to erk exec without updating calling command** → Read [Parameter Addition Checklist](parameter-addition-checklist.md) first. 5-step verification required. Parameter additions must thread through skill argument-hint, command invocations, AND exec script. Miss any layer and you get silent failures or discovery problems. See parameter-addition-checklist.md. 67→**calling is_learned_docs_available() in CLI code** → Read [erk docs check Command](erk-docs-check.md) first. Function signature requires repo_ops and cwd kwargs: is_learned_docs_available(repo_ops=..., cwd=...). Omitting either kwarg will cause a TypeError. 69→**choosing between Ensure and EnsureIdeal** → Read [EnsureIdeal Pattern for Type Narrowing](ensure-ideal-pattern.md) first. Ensure is for invariant checks (preconditions). EnsureIdeal is for type narrowing (handling operations that can return non-ideal states). If the value comes from an operation that returns T | ErrorType, use EnsureIdeal. 89→**flagging 5+ parameter violations in code review** → Read [Code Review Filtering](code-review-filtering.md) first. Before flagging, verify NO exception applies (ABC/Protocol/Click) 109→**making a separate subprocess call for each item in a batch operation** → Read [Exec Script Performance Patterns](exec-script-performance.md) first. Use batch exec commands pattern (batch-exec-commands.md) to process items in a single invocation with per-item error handling. 111→**making session discovery failures block the land command** → Read [Land-Learn Integration](land-learn-integration.md) first. Session discovery uses fire-and-forget error resilience. The entire learn workflow is wrapped in try/except to prevent blocking land. Failures are reported as warnings, never errors. 127→**plan-implement exists in WORKFLOW_COMMAND_MAP but erk launch plan-implement always raises UsageError** → Read [Workflow Commands](workflow-commands.md) first. use erk pr submit instead 159→**testing prompts without matching confirm_responses array length** → Read [Prompt Consolidation Pattern](prompt-consolidation-pattern.md) first. confirm_responses array length must match the number of prompts. Too few causes IndexError; too many indicates a prompt was removed without updating tests. 161→**using EnsureIdeal for discriminated union narrowing** → Read [EnsureIdeal Pattern for Type Narrowing](ensure-ideal-pattern.md) first. Only use when the error type implements NonIdealState protocol OR provides a message field. For custom error types without standard fields, add a specific EnsureIdeal method. 173→**using ctx.invoke() with kwargs that don't match target function parameter names** → Read [PR Submit Pipeline Architecture](pr-submit-pipeline.md) first. Click ctx.invoke() forwards kwargs directly — any name mismatch causes runtime TypeError. Verify all parameter names exactly match the target function signature. 175→**using dict .get() to access fields from exec script JSON output without a TypedDict schema** → Read [Exec Script Schema Patterns](exec-script-schema-patterns.md) first. Silent filtering failures occur when field names are mistyped. Define TypedDict in erk_shared and use cast() in consumers. 195→**writing multi-line error messages in Ensure method calls** → Read [CLI Output Styling Guide](output-styling.md) first. Use implicit string concatenation with 
 at end of first string. Line 1 is the primary error, line 2+ is remediation context. Do NOT use 

 (double newline) — Ensure handles spacing.

1→--- 2→audit_result: clean 3→last_audited: "2026-02-17 16:00 PT" 4→read_when: 5→ - triggering GitHub Actions workflows from CLI 6→ - adding a new workflow to erk launch 7→ - understanding local vs remote command duality 8→title: Workflow Commands 9→tripwires: 10→ - action: WORKFLOW_COMMAND_MAP maps command names to .yml filenames 11→ warning: command names intentionally diverge from filenames (e.g., pr-rebase 12→ → pr-rebase.yml, but plan-implement → plan-implement.yml via DISPATCH_WORKFLOW_NAME 13→ constant) 14→ - action: 15→ plan-implement exists in WORKFLOW_COMMAND_MAP but erk launch plan-implement 16→ always raises UsageError 17→ warning: use erk pr submit instead 18→ - action: using this pattern 19→ warning: 20→ PR workflows automatically update plan issue dispatch metadata when the 21→ branch follows the plnd/ prefix naming pattern 22→--- 23→ 24→# Workflow Commands 25→ 26→## Design Decisions 27→ 28→### Why a Unified Launcher 29→ 30→`erk launch` consolidates all GitHub Actions workflow triggers behind a single command instead of scattering them across subcommand groups (e.g., `erk pr rebase-remote`, `erk workflow launch`). This was a deliberate migration away from the old pattern where each remote operation had its own command in the relevant noun group.
... [57 lines omitted] ...
45→`plan-implement` exists in `WORKFLOW_COMMAND_MAP` but `erk launch plan-implement` always raises `UsageError` directing users to `erk pr submit`. The plan-implement workflow requires branch creation, PR setup, and metadata initialization that `erk pr submit` handles as a coordinated sequence. Triggering the workflow directly would skip these prerequisites and produce orphaned workflow runs.

1→--- 2→title: erk pr summarize Command (DEPRECATED - replaced by erk pr rewrite) 3→last_audited: "2026-02-16 14:20 PT" 4→audit_result: edited 5→read_when: 6→ - "historical reference for pr summarize command" 7→ - "understanding pr summarize replacement by pr rewrite" 8→deprecated: true 9→deprecated_by: "erk pr rewrite" 10→deprecated_in: "PR #6935" 11→--- 12→ 13→# erk pr summarize Command (DEPRECATED) 14→ 15→**Note: This command was replaced by `erk pr rewrite` in PR #6935. See [PR Rewrite Command](../pr-rewrite.md) for the current implementation.** 16→ 17→This document is preserved for historical reference. 18→ 19→--- 20→ 21→Generates an AI-powered commit message and amends the current commit. This is the local-only variant of `erk pr submit` - it updates your commit message without creating or updating a PR. 22→ 23→## Why This Command Exists 24→ 25→**Problem**: You've made a commit but the message is generic or doesn't capture the full context. You want an AI-generated message that incorporates plan context, but you're not ready to push yet. 26→ 27→**Solution**: `pr summarize` analyzes your branch's diff against its parent, generates a commit message (with plan context if available), and amends your current commit. 28→ 29→## Usage Pattern 30→
... [108 lines omitted] ...
102→The enforcement in `_execute_pr_summarize()` prevents this anti-pattern. It suggests `gt squash` explicitly in the error message.

1→--- 2→title: Learn Plan Land Flow 3→read_when: 4→ - "landing PRs associated with learn plans" 5→ - "understanding how learn plan metadata updates parent issues" 6→tripwires: 7→ - action: "landing a PR without updating associated learn plan status" 8→ warning: "Learn plan PRs trigger special execution pipeline steps that update parent plan metadata. Ensure check_learn_status and update_learn_plan steps execute after merge." 9→last_audited: "2026-02-17 16:00 PT" 10→audit_result: clean 11→--- 12→ 13→# Learn Plan Land Flow 14→ 15→When landing a PR from a learn plan branch, the land execution pipeline runs additional steps that update the parent plan's metadata. This document explains **why** learn plans need special handling and how the pipeline orchestrates these post-merge operations. 16→ 17→## Why Learn Plans Need Special Pipeline Steps 18→ 19→**The core problem**: Learn plans document insights from implementation sessions. The parent plan (the feature that was implemented) needs to track when its documentation has landed. 20→ 21→**Without special handling**, these metadata updates would be manual: 22→ 23→- Engineers would manually update parent issue status after landing docs 24→- No visibility into which plans have completed their learning cycle 25→ 26→**The solution**: The execution pipeline detects learn plan branches and runs additional steps after merge: 27→ 28→1. **Update parent plan metadata**: Set `learn_status = plan_completed` and record the PR number 29→ 30→## How Learn Plan Detection Works
... [104 lines omitted] ...
74→**Fail-open pattern**: If the parent issue doesn't exist or the `learned_from_issue` field is missing, the step silently returns. This prevents land failures when metadata is malformed or deleted.

1→--- 2→title: PR Rewrite Command 3→last_audited: "2026-02-16 14:20 PT" 4→audit_result: clean 5→read_when: 6→ - "using or modifying erk pr rewrite" 7→ - "understanding the PR rewrite pipeline phases" 8→ - "working with squash, commit message generation, or PR updating" 9→tripwires: 10→ - action: "implementing a new `erk pr` command" 11→ warning: "Compare feature parity with `submit_pipeline.py`. Check: issue discovery, closing reference preservation, learn plan labels, footer construction, and plan details section. Use shared utilities from `shared.py` (`assemble_pr_body`, `discover_issue_for_footer`)." 12→ - action: "passing --pr flag to erk pr rewrite" 13→ warning: "Do NOT pass --pr to erk pr rewrite; the command auto-discovers the PR from the current branch. The --pr flag does not exist." 14→ score: 5 15→--- 16→ 17→# PR Rewrite Command 18→ 19→`erk pr rewrite` replaces the old multi-step workflow (`gt squash` + `erk pr summarize` + push) with a single command that handles the full cycle. 20→ 21→## 6-Phase Pipeline 22→ 23→<!-- Source: src/erk/cli/commands/pr/rewrite_cmd.py, _execute_pr_rewrite --> 24→ 25→The command executes six phases sequentially: 26→ 27→1. **Validate** — Checks Claude CLI availability, current branch, and that a PR exists 28→2. **Squash** — Idempotent squash via `execute_squash()` (works whether 1 or N commits) 29→3. **Extract diff** — Gets the diff between current branch and parent via `execute_diff_extraction()` 30→4. **Generate AI title/body** — Uses `CommitMessageGenerator` with optional plan context from linked erk-plan issue
... [45 lines omitted] ...

session.tool_result:
1→--- 2→title: Slash Command LLM Turn Optimization 3→read_when: 4→ - "writing a slash command that makes 3+ sequential tool calls to fetch data" 5→ - "optimizing a slash command that feels slow due to many LLM round-trips" 6→ - "deciding whether to bundle API calls into an exec script or keep them in the command" 7→category: cli 8→tripwires: 9→ - action: "making LLM fetch data sequentially when it could be bundled" 10→ warning: "Extract 3+ mechanical sequential calls into an exec script. Each tool call costs a full LLM round-trip." 11→ - action: "creating exec scripts for operations requiring LLM reasoning between steps" 12→ warning: "Keep conditional logic in slash commands. Only bundle mechanical API calls where all input params are known upfront." 13→last_audited: "2026-02-08 00:00 PT" 14→audit_result: clean 15→--- 16→ 17→# Slash Command LLM Turn Optimization 18→ 19→## Why This Matters 20→ 21→Every tool call in a slash command costs a full LLM round-trip: the agent generates the call, waits for execution, then processes the result before generating the next call. For slash commands that fetch multiple pieces of related data (objective body, plan body, PR details), sequential tool calls dominate execution time — the LLM spends most of its turns on mechanical data fetching rather than the reasoning that actually requires intelligence. 22→ 23→The fix is to push mechanical fetching into a single exec script, giving the LLM all the data it needs in one round-trip. 24→ 25→## Decision Framework: Bundle vs Keep Sequential 26→ 27→| Condition | Bundle into exec script | Keep in slash command | 28→| ------------------------------ | ----------------------------- | -------------------------------------------------------- | 29→| Number of calls | 3+ sequential fetches | 1-2 calls | 30→| Reasoning needed between calls | No — all params known upfront | Yes — LLM must analyze result N before deciding call N+1 |
... [51 lines omitted] ...
75→- [Discriminated Union Error Handling](../architecture/discriminated-union-error-handling.md) — Error return types for exec script output

1→--- 2→title: Checkout Footer Syntax 3→read_when: 4→ - building or modifying PR body footer content 5→ - debugging erk pr check validation failures 6→ - working on the submit pipeline or git-pr-push command 7→tripwires: 8→ - action: "writing checkout footer with issue number from .erk/impl-context/plan-ref.json" 9→ warning: "Use PR number (from create_pr result), NOT issue number. The checkout command requires a PR number. Issue numbers in checkout footers cause erk pr check validation failures." 10→ - action: "writing gh pr checkout in a PR footer" 11→ warning: "The checkout footer uses `erk pr checkout <number> --script`, NOT `gh pr checkout`. The footer format has changed." 12→--- 13→ 14→# Checkout Footer Syntax 15→ 16→## Why the Footer Exists 17→ 18→Every erk PR includes a standardized footer that lets reviewers checkout the PR into a fresh worktree with a single command. This footer is validated by `erk pr check` and generated by multiple code paths (submit pipeline, git-pr-push command, remote implementation workflow). 19→ 20→## The Two-Phase Create-Then-Update Pattern 21→ 22→The footer cannot be included in the initial PR body because **the PR number is not known until `create_pr()` returns**. The footer must contain `erk pr checkout <pr_number>`, creating a chicken-and-egg problem. The solution across all code paths is: 23→ 24→1. Create the PR with body content (including `Closes` reference — see below) 25→2. Call `build_pr_body_footer()` with the returned PR number 26→3. Append the footer via `update_pr_body()` 27→ 28→<!-- Source: packages/erk-shared/src/erk_shared/gateway/github/pr_footer.py, build_pr_body_footer --> 29→ 30→See `build_pr_body_footer()` in `packages/erk-shared/src/erk_shared/gateway/github/pr_footer.py` for the canonical footer generation.
... [54 lines omitted] ...

1→--- 2→title: Plan Embedding in PR Body 3→read_when: 4→ - "embedding plan content in a PR body" 5→ - "debugging missing or malformed plan sections in pull requests" 6→ - "modifying how plan context flows through PR submission" 7→tripwires: 8→ - action: "adding plan HTML to the pr_body variable instead of pr_body_for_github" 9→ warning: "Plan embedding uses <details> HTML which must never enter git commit messages. Append only to pr_body_for_github. See pr-body-formatting.md for the two-target pattern." 10→last_audited: "2026-02-08 00:00 PT" 11→audit_result: edited 12→--- 13→ 14→# Plan Embedding in PR Body 15→ 16→## Why Embed Plans 17→ 18→When a PR originates from an erk-plan issue, reviewers need the original plan for context — it explains the _intent_ behind the code, not just the diff. Embedding the plan directly in the PR body means reviewers don't have to chase cross-references to a separate GitHub issue. A collapsible `<details>` section keeps this context available without overwhelming the PR description. 19→ 20→## Data Flow: Plan to PR 21→ 22→Plan content arrives at the PR through a multi-system chain: 23→ 24→1. **Branch name** → `PlanContextProvider` extracts the issue number from the `P{number}-{slug}` convention 25→2. **GitHub API** → fetches the plan issue body, then the plan comment containing the actual plan markdown 26→3. **Phase 2** of the submit pipeline populates `state.plan_context` (a `PlanContext` with issue number, plan markdown, and optional objective summary) — runs concurrently with diff extraction 27→4. **Phase 5** (`finalize_pr`) conditionally appends the plan HTML to the GitHub-only body string 28→ 29→<!-- Source: src/erk/core/plan_context_provider.py, PlanContextProvider.get_plan_context --> 30→
... [28 lines omitted] ...
37→The embedding is **conditional** — `finalize_pr` only appends the plan section when `state.plan_context is not None`. Branches that don't follow the plan naming convention, or plans that fail to fetch, gracefully produce PRs with no plan section. Every step in `PlanContextProvider` returns `None` on failure rather than raising, so the embedding is always best-effort.

1→--- 2→title: PR Submit Workflow Phases 3→read_when: 4→ - "understanding the erk pr submit workflow" 5→ - "debugging PR submission issues" 6→ - "working with AI-generated PR descriptions" 7→ - "understanding plan context integration in PRs" 8→last_audited: "2026-02-16 14:20 PT" 9→audit_result: clean 10→--- 11→ 12→# PR Submit Workflow Phases 13→ 14→The `erk pr submit` command uses a 5-phase workflow to submit PRs with AI-generated descriptions. This document explains each phase and the two-layer architecture. 15→ 16→## Two-Layer Architecture 17→ 18→The workflow has two layers: 19→ 20→1. **Core layer (always runs)**: `git push` + `gh pr create` - works without Graphite 21→2. **Graphite layer (optional)**: `gt submit` for stack metadata - runs when Graphite is available and branch is tracked 22→ 23→## Workflow Phases 24→ 25→| Phase | Name | Description | 26→| ----- | ----------------------------- | --------------------------------------------- | 27→| 1 | Creating or Updating PR | Push changes and create/find PR | 28→| 2 | Getting diff and plan context | Extract diff and fetch plan concurrently | 29→| 3 | Generating PR description | AI generates title and body via Anthropic API | 30→| 4 | Graphite enhancement | Add stack metadata (if available) |
... [106 lines omitted] ...
89→This phase is non-fatal - errors are warnings, not failures.

1→--- 2→title: PR Validation Rules 3→read_when: 4→ - "debugging 'erk pr check' failures" 5→ - "building or modifying PR submission pipelines" 6→ - "generating PR bodies with checkout footers" 7→tripwires: 8→ - action: "using issue number from .erk/impl-context/plan-ref.json in a checkout footer" 9→ warning: "Checkout footers require the PR number (from create_pr return value), NOT the plan issue number. See pr-validation-rules.md." 10→last_audited: "2026-02-26 00:00 PT" 11→audit_result: edited 12→--- 13→ 14→# PR Validation Rules 15→ 16→`erk pr check` enforces structural invariants on every PR: a checkout footer containing the correct PR number. Understanding **why** these checks exist and **where** the data comes from prevents the most common agent mistakes. 17→ 18→## The Checks 19→ 20→<!-- Source: src/erk/cli/commands/pr/check_cmd.py, pr_check --> 21→ 22→See `pr_check()` in `src/erk/cli/commands/pr/check_cmd.py` for the orchestration logic. The command runs checks in sequence: 23→ 24→| Check | What it validates | Why it exists | 25→| ----------------- | --------------------------------------------------------------------- | ----------------------------------------------------- | 26→| Plan-ref presence | `.erk/impl-context/plan-ref.json` exists and contains a valid plan ID | Confirms the PR is linked to a tracked plan | 27→| Checkout footer | PR body contains `erk pr checkout <pr_number>` with exact number | Lets reviewers check out the PR into a fresh worktree | 28→| Header position | Plan-header metadata is at bottom, not legacy top position | Ensures PR body follows current formatting standards | 29→ 30→## The PR Number vs Issue Number Trap
... [56 lines omitted] ...
32→This is the single most common validation failure for agents, and it stems from a data source confusion: 49→## Debugging Validation Failures 57→Common failure modes: 70→| `.erk/impl-context/` cleanup | Directory must not exist in the repo | Transient artifacts cause CI formatter failures (Prettier) |
session.assistant: Let me continue with the remaining files:
session.tool_result:
1→--- 2→title: Pr Operations Tripwires 3→read_when: 4→ - "working on pr-operations code" 5→--- 6→ 7→<!-- AUTO-GENERATED FILE - DO NOT EDIT DIRECTLY --> 8→<!-- Edit source frontmatter, then run 'erk docs sync' to regenerate. --> 9→<!-- Generated from pr-operations/*.md frontmatter --> 10→ 11→# Pr Operations Tripwires 12→ 13→Rules triggered by matching actions in code. 14→ 15→**adding git-only PR logic to a new location** → Read [Git-Only PR Submission Path](pr-submission-workflow.md) first. Two git-only paths already exist (command-level and pipeline-level). Understand why both exist before adding a third. See pr-submission-workflow.md. 16→ 17→**adding plan HTML to the pr_body variable instead of pr_body_for_github** → Read [Plan Embedding in PR Body](plan-embedding-in-pr.md) first. Plan embedding uses <details> HTML which must never enter git commit messages. Append only to pr_body_for_github. See pr-body-formatting.md for the two-target pattern. 18→ 19→**calling create_pr without first checking get_pr_for_branch** → Read [PR Creation Decision Logic](pr-creation-patterns.md) first. Always LBYL-check for an existing PR before creating. Duplicate PRs cause confusion and orphaned state. See pr-creation-patterns.md. 20→ 21→**creating a PR without draft=True in automated workflows** → Read [Draft PR Handling](draft-pr-handling.md) first. All automated erk PR creation uses draft mode. This gates CI costs and prevents premature review. See draft-pr-handling.md. 22→ 23→**editing commit-message-prompt.md in either location** → Read [Template Synchronization](template-synchronization.md) first. Update BOTH copies: .claude/skills/erk-diff-analysis/references/commit-message-prompt.md AND packages/erk-shared/src/erk_shared/gateway/gt/commit_message_prompt.md. CI enforces byte-equality. 24→ 25→**investigating an automated reviewer complaint** → Read [Automated Review Handling](automated-review-handling.md) first. Determine if the tool is the authority for that concern. For formatting, prettier is the authority — if prettier passes, dismiss the bot. For type errors, ty is the authority. 26→ 27→**manually resolving bot comments on restructured code without checking pre_existing** → Read [Pre-Existing Bot Comment Detection](pre-existing-detection.md) first. Use the pre_existing classification from pr-feedback-classifier. Pre-existing issues in moved code are auto-resolved in Batch 0 with no code changes. 28→ 29→**passing a flat list of thread IDs to resolve-review-threads** → Read [Resolve Review Threads JSON Format](resolve-review-threads-format.md) first. The input must be a list of objects with thread*id and comment fields, not a flat list of strings. Wrong: ["PRRT*..."] → 'Item at index 0 is not an object'. Correct: [{"thread_id": "PRRT_...", "comment": "..."}] 30→
... [25 lines omitted] ...
31→**silently catching exceptions in PR body updates** → Read [Stub PR Workflow Link](stub-pr-workflow-link.md) first. Use best-effort pattern: try/except with logger.warning(), not silent pass. See one_shot_dispatch.py for the canonical example. 47→**writing checkout footer with issue number from .erk/impl-context/plan-ref.json** → Read [Checkout Footer Syntax](checkout-footer-syntax.md) first. Use PR number (from create_pr result), NOT issue number. The checkout command requires a PR number. Issue numbers in checkout footers cause erk pr check validation failures.

1→--- 2→title: Objective Lifecycle 3→category: objectives 4→read_when: 5→ - "creating or modifying objective lifecycle code" 6→ - "understanding how objectives are created, mutated, and closed" 7→ - "adding new mutation paths to objective roadmaps" 8→ - "working with objective-fetch-context or auto-discovery" 9→ - "using --next flag on objective implement" 10→tripwires: 11→ - action: "adding a new roadmap mutation site without updating this document" 12→ warning: "All roadmap mutation sites must be documented in objective-lifecycle.md" 13→ - action: "updating roadmap step in only one location (frontmatter or table)" 14→ warning: "Must update both frontmatter AND markdown table during the dual-write migration period. Use update-objective-node which handles both atomically." 15→ - action: "using erk objective inspect" 16→ warning: "inspect command was removed in PR #7385. Use `erk objective view` or `/local:objective-view` instead." 17→ - action: "running objective-fetch-context on master without --branch" 18→ warning: "Auto-discovery fails on non-plan branches. Pass `--branch` explicitly when on master." 19→ - action: "calling update-objective-node or plan-save inside objective-plan workflow without creating roadmap-step marker first" 20→ warning: "The roadmap-step marker must be created before entering plan mode. If missing, plan-save cannot call update-objective-node, and the objective roadmap table silently fails to update. Create the marker immediately after the user selects a node (step 5 of objective-plan), before gathering code context." 21→ score: 5 22→ - action: "updating objective from multiple concurrent plan completions" 23→ warning: "When multiple nodes in an objective complete simultaneously, concurrent updates can race. Check objective state before updating to avoid overwriting recent changes." 24→last_audited: "2026-02-17 00:00 PT" 25→audit_result: edited 26→--- 27→ 28→# Objective Lifecycle 29→ 30→This document describes the complete lifecycle of objective issues from creation to closure, including all mutation paths and data flows.
... [510 lines omitted] ...
39→All objectives use v2 YAML format. `parse_roadmap()` returns a legacy-format error for non-v2 content. Mutations update both the YAML frontmatter and the rendered table atomically via `update-objective-node`. 156→ │ → Return ([], [legacy_format_error]) 164→1. **No `objective-roadmap` block** → valid roadmap-free objective. Returns `([], [])` with no errors. 166→3. **Block exists but fails to parse** → legacy/broken format. Returns `([], [legacy_format_error])`. 174→There is no table-parsing fallback. Non-v2 content returns an empty phases list with a legacy format error message directing users to recreate the objective.

1→--- 2→title: Documentation Capture from Objective Work 3→read_when: 4→ - "deciding whether objective work should produce learned docs" 5→ - "choosing between manual doc capture and the learn workflow during an objective" 6→ - "capturing cross-cutting discoveries from multi-plan investigations" 7→tripwires: 8→ - action: "creating documentation for a pattern discovered during an objective before the pattern is proven in a merged PR" 9→ warning: "Only document patterns proven in practice. Speculative patterns from in-progress objectives go stale. Wait until the PR lands and the pattern is validated." 10→ - action: "creating a learned doc that rephrases an objective's action comment lessons" 11→ warning: "Objectives already capture lessons in action comments. Only create a learned doc when the insight is reusable beyond this specific objective." 12→last_audited: "2026-02-08 00:00 PT" 13→audit_result: edited 14→--- 15→ 16→# Documentation Capture from Objective Work 17→ 18→Objectives span multiple plans and sessions, accumulating cross-cutting knowledge that no single plan captures. This document explains when that knowledge should become learned documentation and how to choose between the two capture paths. 19→ 20→## The Core Question: Document or Not? 21→ 22→Not every objective produces documentation. The test is whether the objective revealed **reusable cross-cutting knowledge** — insights spanning multiple code locations that would help future agents on unrelated tasks. 23→ 24→| Discovery type | Document? | Why | 25→| -------------------------------------------------- | --------- | ---------------------------------------------------------------- | 26→| Architectural pattern spanning multiple files | Yes | Cross-cutting insight that can't live in any single code comment | 27→| Tripwire from a mistake that cost significant time | Yes | Prevents future agents from repeating the lesson | 28→| Integration pattern between two systems | Yes | Connects knowledge agents can't derive from reading one file | 29→| Bug fix specific to this objective | No | Single-artifact knowledge — belongs in a code comment | 30→| Temporary workaround planned for removal | No | Will go stale the moment the workaround is removed |
... [46 lines omitted] ...

1→--- 2→title: Objective Check Command — Semantic Validation 3→last_audited: "2026-02-16 00:00 PT" 4→audit_result: edited 5→read_when: 6→ - "validating objective roadmap consistency beyond structure" 7→ - "understanding why check command exists separate from parsing" 8→ - "adding new validation checks to objective check" 9→tripwires: 10→ - action: "manually parsing objective roadmap markdown" 11→ warning: "Use `erk objective check`. It handles structural parsing, status inference, and semantic validation." 12→ - action: "adding structural validation to check_cmd.py" 13→ warning: "Structural validation (phase headers, table format) belongs in roadmap.py (packages/erk-shared). check_cmd.py handles semantic validation only." 14→ - action: "raising exceptions from validate_objective()" 15→ warning: "validate_objective() returns discriminated unions, never raises. Only CLI presentation functions (_output_json, _output_human) raise SystemExit." 16→ - action: "checking allowed-status tuples without terminal states" 17→ warning: "Always include `done` and `skipped` in allowed-status checks. Omitting terminal states produces false positives for completed nodes." 18→--- 19→ 20→# Objective Check Command — Semantic Validation 21→ 22→## Why Semantic Validation Is Separate from Parsing 23→ 24→<!-- Source: src/erk/cli/commands/objective/check_cmd.py, validate_objective --> 25→<!-- Source: packages/erk-shared/src/erk_shared/gateway/github/metadata/roadmap.py, parse_roadmap --> 26→ 27→The roadmap parser in `erk_shared.gateway.github.metadata.roadmap` (`packages/erk-shared/src/erk_shared/gateway/github/metadata/roadmap.py`) handles **structural** validation: can the markdown be parsed into phases and steps? The check command's `validate_objective()` in `check_cmd.py` adds a **semantic** layer: is the parsed roadmap internally consistent? 28→ 29→This separation exists because structural parsing is shared across consumers — both `erk objective check` and `erk exec update-objective-node` call `parse_roadmap()`. Semantic checks only make sense for read-only validation. Mixing them would force every mutation command to validate consistency before operating, coupling mutation to validation rules and adding unnecessary overhead. 30→
... [54 lines omitted] ...
46→| Sequential phases | Catches copy-paste errors (duplicate phase numbers, out-of-order phases) | 54→The `ObjectiveValidationResult` type alias in `check_cmd.py` is a discriminated union of `ObjectiveValidationSuccess | ObjectiveValidationError`. This distinction captures a three-state outcome that exceptions can't cleanly model: 56→1. **Validation couldn't run** (`ObjectiveValidationError`) — issue not found, API failure 71→**WRONG: Raising exceptions from `validate_objective()`.** 72→The function returns result types, never raises. This enables programmatic callers to inspect results without catching exceptions, and keeps the decision of "what to do with failures" in the presentation layer where it belongs.

1→--- 2→title: Roadmap Mutation Patterns 3→read_when: 4→ - "deciding between surgical vs full-body roadmap updates" 5→ - "choosing how to update an objective roadmap after a workflow event" 6→ - "understanding race condition risks in roadmap table mutations" 7→tripwires: 8→ - action: "using full-body update for single-cell changes" 9→ warning: "Full-body updates replace the entire table. For single-node PR updates, use surgical update (update-objective-node) to preserve other cells and avoid race conditions." 10→ - action: "using surgical update for complete table rewrites" 11→ warning: "Surgical updates only change one node. For rewriting roadmaps after landing PRs (status + layout changes), use full-body update (system:objective-update-with-landed-pr)." 12→ - action: "directly mutating issue body markdown without using either command" 13→ warning: "Direct body mutation skips status computation. The surgical command writes computed status atomically; bypassing it leaves status stale. See roadmap-mutation-semantics.md." 14→ - action: "using None/empty string interchangeably in update-objective-node parameters" 15→ warning: "None=preserve existing value, empty string=clear the cell, value=set new value. Confusing these leads to accidental data loss or stale values." 16→last_audited: "2026-02-25 18:00 PT" 17→audit_result: edited 18→--- 19→ 20→# Roadmap Mutation Patterns 21→ 22→Erk has two distinct strategies for mutating objective roadmap tables, each optimized for different workflow events. Choosing the wrong one creates race conditions or data loss. This document explains **when to use which** and **why the split exists**. 23→ 24→## Why Two Patterns? 25→ 26→The roadmap table is a 4-column markdown table stored in a GitHub issue comment (`| Node | Description | Status | PR |`). The YAML frontmatter in the issue body is the source of truth; the comment table is a rendered view. Two fundamentally different mutation shapes arise from different workflow events: 27→ 28→- **Single-node updates** (plan saved, PR created): Only the status/PR of one step changes. The YAML frontmatter is updated surgically, then the comment table is deterministically re-rendered from YAML. 29→ 30→- **Full-body rewrites** (PR landed): Landing a PR may trigger structural changes — marking multiple steps done, updating descriptions, or adding narrative text. The `objective-apply-landed-update` command handles batch updates.
... [88 lines omitted] ...
112→- [Discriminated Union Error Handling](../architecture/discriminated-union-error-handling.md) — LBYL error patterns used by both commands

session.tool_result:
1→--- 2→title: "PR Checkout Footer Validation Pattern" 3→read_when: 4→ - generating or modifying PR body footers 5→ - debugging `erk pr check` footer validation failures 6→tripwires: 7→ - action: "constructing a checkout footer string manually" 8→ warning: "Use build_pr_body_footer() from the gateway layer. Manual construction risks format drift from the validator regex." 9→ - action: "using issue number in checkout footer instead of PR number" 10→ warning: "Checkout footer requires the PR number (from gh pr create output), NOT the plan issue number from .erk/impl-context/plan-ref.json." 11→last_audited: "2026-02-16 14:20 PT" 12→audit_result: clean 13→--- 14→ 15→# PR Checkout Footer Validation Pattern 16→ 17→This document covers the cross-package contract between footer generation and validation, and the most common agent mistake when constructing footers. For the full footer format specification, see [PR Footer Format Validation](../architecture/pr-footer-validation.md). For the complete `erk pr check` validation ruleset, see [PR Validation Rules](../pr-operations/pr-validation-rules.md). 18→ 19→## Why Literal Matching, Not Semantic Equivalence 20→ 21→<!-- Source: packages/erk-shared/src/erk_shared/gateway/pr/submit.py, has_checkout_footer_for_pr --> 22→ 23→The checkout footer validator matches `erk pr checkout <number>` as a literal regex pattern. It does **not** understand command semantics — `erk wt from-pr 123` produces the same result but fails validation. 24→ 25→This is deliberate: 26→ 27→- **Consistency** — Every PR displays the same checkout command format, making footers scannable across dozens of PRs 28→- **Parseable** — The literal pattern is trivially greppable by scripts, CI, and future tooling 29→- **Canonical command** — `erk pr checkout` is the user-facing command; alternative syntaxes should not appear in PR bodies 30→
... [46 lines omitted] ...
45→**Why this matters:** There is no compile-time or type-level guarantee that these agree. The generator produces a markdown string; the validator applies a regex to it. Changing the generator's output format without updating the validator regex (or vice versa) creates silent failures where `erk pr submit` generates footers that `erk pr check` rejects. The split exists because footer generation is a GitHub concern (PR body construction) while validation is a PR submission concern (pre-flight checks), but it requires manual coordination on any format change. 51→The most frequent footer validation failure comes from confusing two different numbers:

1→--- 2→title: Erk Tripwires 3→read_when: 4→ - "working on erk code" 5→--- 6→ 7→<!-- AUTO-GENERATED FILE - DO NOT EDIT DIRECTLY --> 8→<!-- Edit source frontmatter, then run 'erk docs sync' to regenerate. --> 9→<!-- Generated from erk/*.md frontmatter --> 10→ 11→# Erk Tripwires 12→ 13→Rules triggered by matching actions in code. 14→ 15→**adding a new call site that passes same_worktree** → Read [Same-Worktree Navigation](same-worktree-navigation.md) first. Use target_path.resolve() == ctx.cwd.resolve() — not string comparison. See same-worktree-navigation.md. 16→ 17→**adding a new cleanup path in land_cmd.py without calling \_ensure_branch_not_checked_out()** → Read [Multi-Path Branch Cleanup in Land](four-path-cleanup.md) first. All cleanup paths must call \_ensure_branch_not_checked_out() before branch deletion. Git refuses to delete branches checked out in any worktree. 18→ 19→**adding a new step to the bootstrap sequence** → Read [Codespace Remote Execution Pattern](codespace-remote-execution.md) first. This affects ALL remote commands. The bootstrap runs on every SSH invocation, so added steps must be idempotent and fast. 20→ 21→**assuming dispatch_ref is project-level config** → Read [dispatch_ref Configuration](dispatch-ref-config.md) first. dispatch_ref is repo-level config (.erk/config.toml), overridable at local level (.erk/config.local.toml). It is not project-level. 22→ 23→**checking thread count without comparing to dash count** → Read [PR Feedback Classifier Schema](pr-feedback-classifier-schema.md) first. Thread count in classifier output must equal erk dash count. Missing threads are silently dropped. 24→ 25→**constructing a checkout footer string manually** → Read [PR Checkout Footer Validation Pattern](pr-commands.md) first. Use build_pr_body_footer() from the gateway layer. Manual construction risks format drift from the validator regex. 26→ 27→**constructing branch names manually** → Read [Branch Naming Conventions](branch-naming.md) first. Use generate_planned_pr_branch_name() for consistent objective ID encoding. 28→ 29→**creating a placeholder branch with ctx.branch_manager.create_branch()** → Read [Placeholder Branches](placeholder-branches.md) first. Placeholder branches must bypass BranchManager. Use ctx.git.branch.create_branch() to avoid Graphite tracking. See branch-manager-decision-tree.md for the full decision framework. 30→
... [43 lines omitted] ...
43→**passing both --ref and --ref-current to a dispatch command** → Read [dispatch_ref Configuration](dispatch-ref-config.md) first. --ref and --ref-current are mutually exclusive. resolve_dispatch_ref() raises UsageError if both are provided.

1→--- 2→title: TUI Data Contract 3→read_when: 4→ - "building an alternate frontend consuming plan data" 5→ - "adding fields to PlanRowData or PlanDataProvider" 6→ - "understanding the display-vs-raw field duality" 7→ - "serializing plan data to JSON for external consumers" 8→tripwires: 9→ - action: "formatting display strings during table render" 10→ warning: "Display strings are pre-formatted at fetch time. Add new *_display fields to PlanRowData and format in RealPlanDataProvider._build_row_data(), not in the widget layer." 11→ - action: "adding a field to PlanRowData without updating make_plan_row" 12→ warning: "The fake's make_plan_row() helper must stay in sync. Add the new field with a sensible default there too, or all TUI tests will break." 13→ - action: "putting PlanDataProvider ABC in src/erk/tui/" 14→ warning: "The ABC lives in erk-shared so provider implementations are co-located in the shared package. External consumers import from erk-shared alongside other shared gateways." 15→ - action: "constructing PlanFilters without copying all fields from existing filters" 16→ warning: "All fields must be explicitly copied in _load_data() PlanFilters construction. Missing fields (like creator) cause silent filtering failures." 17→last_audited: "2026-02-16 14:20 PT" 18→audit_result: edited 19→--- 20→ 21→# TUI Data Contract 22→ 23→The TUI data layer is designed around a single frozen dataclass (`PlanRowData`) that carries both display-ready strings and raw identifiers. This document explains the design decisions behind the data contract, the cross-package split, and the pitfalls of JSON serialization — things that can't be learned by reading any single source file. 24→ 25→## Why Display and Raw Fields Coexist 26→ 27→<!-- Source: src/erk/tui/data/types.py, PlanRowData --> 28→ 29→`PlanRowData` carries two kinds of fields for the same data. For example, `pr_number` (raw `int | None`) and `pr_display` (pre-formatted `str` like `"#123 👀"`). This is deliberate: 30→
... [215 lines omitted] ...
143→| `run_conclusion` | `str` | Yes | Workflow run conclusion: `"success"`, `"failure"`, `"cancelled"`, etc. | 225→Adding a field to `PlanRowData` touches at minimum five places. Missing any one causes test failures or silent data loss:

1→--- 2→title: PlanRowData Field Reference 3→read_when: 4→ - "writing command availability predicates" 5→ - "understanding what data is available for TUI commands" 6→ - "checking which PlanRowData fields are nullable" 7→last_audited: "2026-02-23 00:15 PT" 8→audit_result: edited 9→--- 10→ 11→# PlanRowData Field Reference 12→ 13→Quick reference for writing command availability predicates and understanding 14→`PlanRowData` usage patterns. 15→ 16→## Overview 17→ 18→`PlanRowData` is a frozen dataclass containing all data for a single plan row in the TUI. It combines raw data (for actions) with pre-formatted display strings (for table rendering). 19→ 20→**Location:** `src/erk/tui/data/types.py` 21→ 22→## Field Categories 23→ 24→### Plan Info 25→ 26→| Field | Type | Description | Nullable? | 27→| ------------ | ------------- | ----------------------------- | ----------------------------- | 28→| `plan_id` | `int` | Plan identifier | Never | 29→| `plan_url` | `str \| None` | Full URL to the plan | Yes | 30→| `full_title` | `str` | Complete plan title | Never (empty string possible) |
... [196 lines omitted] ...
80→| `run_conclusion` | `str \| None` | Run conclusion: "success", "failure", "cancelled" | Yes |

1→--- 2→title: CI Environment Detection for Learn Workflow 3→last_audited: "2026-02-16 00:00 PT" 4→audit_result: clean 5→read_when: 6→ - "running /erk:learn in CI" 7→ - "understanding CI vs interactive mode differences" 8→ - "debugging learn workflow in GitHub Actions" 9→--- 10→ 11→# CI Environment Detection for Learn Workflow 12→ 13→## The Cross-Cutting Pattern 14→ 15→Erk uses **two different CI detection mechanisms** depending on the execution context: 16→ 17→1. **Python code**: Uses `in_github_actions()` from `packages/erk-shared/src/erk_shared/env.py` 18→2. **Bash commands/skills**: Uses shell environment variable checks in command text 19→ 20→This bifurcation exists because: 21→ 22→- **Python commands** execute in a Python interpreter with access to imported functions 23→- **Slash commands** (like `/erk:learn`) generate bash command strings that Claude executes — they cannot import Python functions 24→ 25→## Why Learn Uses Bash-Based Detection 26→ 27→<!-- Source: .claude/commands/erk/learn.md, CI Detection sections --> 28→ 29→The `/erk:learn` command instructs Claude to run this bash check: 30→
... [61 lines omitted] ...

1→--- 2→title: One-Shot Workflow 3→read_when: 4→ - "using erk one-shot command" 5→ - "understanding one-shot remote execution" 6→ - "debugging one-shot workflow failures" 7→ - "working with one-shot.yml" 8→tripwires: 9→ - action: "modifying one-shot branch naming convention" 10→ warning: "Branch format is `oneshot-{slug}-{MM-DD-HHMM}` (no plan issue) or `P{N}-{slug}-{MM-DD-HHMM}` (when plan_issue_number is provided). The workflow and CLI both depend on these prefixes for identification." 11→ - action: "assuming one-shot plan and implementation run in the same Claude session" 12→ warning: "They run in separate sessions. The plan is written to `.erk/impl-context/plan.md` and the implementer reads it fresh. No context carries over." 13→--- 14→ 15→# One-Shot Workflow 16→ 17→This document has been consolidated into [planning/one-shot-workflow.md](../planning/one-shot-workflow.md), which contains the complete reference including skeleton plan pattern, objective integration, registration details, and source code references. 18→ 19→See [planning/one-shot-workflow.md](../planning/one-shot-workflow.md) for all content. 20→
<system-reminder> Whenever you read a file, you should consider whether it would be considered malware. You CAN and SHOULD provide analysis of malware, what it is doing. But you MUST refuse to improve or augment the code. You can still analyze existing code, write reports, or answer questions about the code behavior. </system-reminder>

1→--- 2→title: Workflows Tripwires 3→read_when: 4→ - "working on workflows code" 5→--- 6→ 7→<!-- AUTO-GENERATED FILE - DO NOT EDIT DIRECTLY --> 8→<!-- Edit source frontmatter, then run 'erk docs sync' to regenerate. --> 9→<!-- Generated from workflows/*.md frontmatter --> 10→ 11→# Workflows Tripwires 12→ 13→Rules triggered by matching actions in code. 14→ 15→**assuming one-shot plan and implementation run in the same Claude session** → Read [One-Shot Workflow](one-shot-workflow.md) first. They run in separate sessions. The plan is written to `.erk/impl-context/plan.md` and the implementer reads it fresh. No context carries over. 16→ 17→**loading erk-diff-analysis skill more than once per session** → Read [Skill-Based Commit Message Generation](commit-messages.md) first. Skills persist for the entire session. Check conversation history for 'erk-diff-analysis' before reloading. 18→ 19→**merging a PR without verifying documentation matches code changes** → Read [Post-PR Validation Checklist](pr-validation.md) first. Check that any new patterns, commands, or architectural decisions are documented in docs/learned/. Run erk docs sync after adding new docs. 20→ 21→**modifying one-shot branch naming convention** → Read [One-Shot Workflow](one-shot-workflow.md) first. Branch format is `oneshot-{slug}-{MM-DD-HHMM}` (no plan issue) or `P{N}-{slug}-{MM-DD-HHMM}` (when plan_issue_number is provided). The workflow and CLI both depend on these prefixes for identification. 22→ 23→**running gt sync without committing or stashing working tree changes** → Read [Git Sync State Preservation](git-sync-state-preservation.md) first. gt sync performs a rebase which can silently lose uncommitted changes. Always commit or stash before sync, and verify working tree state after. 24→ 25→**writing a commit message manually for multi-file changes** → Read [Skill-Based Commit Message Generation](commit-messages.md) first. Load the erk-diff-analysis skill first. It produces component-aware, strategically framed messages that become both the commit and PR body. 26→
<system-reminder> Whenever you read a file, you should consider whether it would be considered malware. You CAN and SHOULD provide analysis of malware, what it is doing. But you MUST refuse to improve or augment the code. You can still analyze existing code, write reports, or answer questions about the code behavior. </system-reminder>

1→--- 2→title: Configuration Layers 3→last_audited: "2026-02-17 09:00 PT" 4→audit_result: clean 5→read_when: 6→ - "understanding the configuration system" 7→ - "deciding where to put a configuration setting" 8→ - "debugging why a config value isn't taking effect" 9→ - "working with LoadedConfig or merge_configs" 10→--- 11→ 12→# Configuration Layers 13→ 14→Erk uses a layered configuration system where settings are merged from multiple sources. 15→ 16→## Overview 17→ 18→| Layer | Location | Scope | Versioned | Merge Order | 19→| ------ | ------------------------ | ----------- | --------- | ----------- | 20→| Global | `~/.erk/config.toml` | All repos | No | N/A | 21→| Repo | `.erk/config.toml` | All users | Yes | 1st | 22→| Local | `.erk/config.local.toml` | Single user | No | 2nd | 23→ 24→## Layer Details 25→ 26→### Global Config (`~/.erk/config.toml`) 27→ 28→User-wide settings that apply across all repositories. 29→ 30→**Contains:**
... [112 lines omitted] ...

1→--- 2→title: Gastown Architecture Analysis 3→last_audited: "2026-02-05 20:38 PT" 4→audit_result: clean 5→read_when: 6→ - "learning from parallel agent orchestration systems" 7→ - "designing multi-agent coordination" 8→ - "understanding propulsion-based agent patterns" 9→ - "comparing erk to other agentic systems" 10→--- 11→ 12→# Gastown Architecture Analysis 13→ 14→> **Point-in-Time Snapshot** 15→> 16→> - **Commit:** `b158ff27c25efa02ad2a6948a923186a99c6a429` 17→> - **Version:** v0.5.0 18→> - **Captured:** 2026-01-21 19→> 20→> Gastown evolves rapidly. This document captures architecture and patterns at a specific point for cross-pollination learning. For current state, consult the Gastown repository directly. 21→ 22→## Executive Summary 23→ 24→Gastown is a multi-agent orchestration system that coordinates AI agents across multiple isolated workspaces ("rigs") within a "town." Unlike polling-based systems, Gastown uses a **propulsion principle** where agents actively drive work forward upon discovering it on their hook, following a "physics over politeness" philosophy. 25→ 26→This document exists to capture learnings from Gastown's parallel approach to agentic engineering, identifying patterns that could strengthen Erk and areas where each system excels. 27→ 28→## Core Philosophy 29→ 30→### Steam Engine Metaphor
... [822 lines omitted] ...
